merger_description:
Tasmea Limited (**Tasmea**) is an Australian diversified specialist trade‑skill services group. It owns and operates 26 specialist subsidiaries providing maintenance, shutdown, breakdown and brownfield upgrade services to fixed plant for essential asset owners, primarily in the resources, energy and utilities sectors. Tasmea’s electrical division or segment delivers industrial electrical, instrumentation and electrification services, principally in Western Australia, as well as Queensland, South Australia and the Northern Territory.

Maxim Group Australia Pty Ltd (**Maxim**) is headquartered in Alphington Victoria and operates through Maxim Electrical Services Pty Ltd and Maxim Infrastructure Pty Ltd. Maxim delivers project and construction based electrical works for data centre owners, Tier 1 builders and head contractors, including major Victorian government infrastructure programs. Maxim’s services are principally acquired by data centres, transport infrastructure operators and other commercial sector projects.

Pursuant to a Share Sale and Purchase Agreement (**SSPA**), Tasmea will acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Maxim (together with its subsidiaries) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Tasmea Limited (Tasmea) applied for a notification waiver in respect
of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Maxim
Group Australia Pty Ltd (Maxim), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Tasmea, is an Australian diversified specialist
trade‑skill services group. It owns and operates 26 specialist
subsidiaries providing maintenance, shutdown, breakdown and
brownfield upgrade services to fixed plant for essential asset
owners, primarily in the resources, energy and utilities sectors.
Tasmea’s electrical division or segment delivers industrial electrical,
instrumentation and electrification services, principally in Western
Australia, as well as Queensland, South Australia and the Northern
Territory.
The target, Maxim, headquartered in Alphington Victoria and
operates through Maxim Electrical Services Pty Ltd and Maxim
Infrastructure Pty Ltd. Maxim delivers project and construction
based electrical works for data centre owners, Tier 1 builders and
head contractors, including major Victorian government
infrastructure programs. Maxim’s services are principally acquired
by data centres, transport infrastructure operators and other
commercial sector projects.
The parties both supply electrical services and maintenance across
Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is limited horizontal overlap between Tasmea and
Maxim in the supply of electrical services and maintenance
across Australia, as their respective offering service
different use cases and geographic regions
b. there are alternative suppliers of the types of electrical
services and maintenance offered by the parties in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
